article.title: Artemis II Lifts Off from Kennedy Space Center, Sending Four Astronauts on Humanity's First Lunar Voyage in 53 Years
article.summary: NASA's SLS rocket launched at 6:24 p.m. EDT on April 1, carrying four astronauts on a 10-day free-return trajectory around the Moon and setting multiple historic firsts.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

NASA's Space Launch System rocket lifted off from Launch Pad 39B at Kennedy Space Center on April 1, targeting a 6:24 p.m. EDT launch within a [two-hour window](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1), carrying four astronauts on a 10-day mission around the Moon. Artemis II marks the first crewed voyage beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in December 1972, as [previously reported](/article/2026-03/29-artemis-ii-crew-arrives-at-kennedy-space-center-as-countdown-begins-for-first-crewed-lunar-voyage-in-53-years) when the crew arrived at the launch site.

The mission sends Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, Mission Specialist Christina Koch, and Canadian Space Agency Mission Specialist Jeremy Hansen on a [free-return trajectory around the Moon](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/30/nasas-artemis-ii-launch-mission-countdown-begins/) that will carry them approximately [685,000 miles over 10 days](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1) before returning to Earth for a Pacific Ocean splashdown.

## Countdown and Fueling

The countdown clock began ticking at [4:44 p.m. EDT on March 30](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/30/nasas-artemis-ii-launch-mission-countdown-begins/), with engineers powering up flight hardware, checking communication links, and preparing the rocket's cryogenic systems. On launch day, tanking operations filled the SLS core stage with [approximately 700,000 gallons of liquid oxygen and liquid hydrogen](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates/), while the Interim Cryogenic Propulsion Stage upper stage completed its own fueling sequence.

Launch Director Charlie Blackwell-Thompson [gave the official 'go' for tanking](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates/) as the countdown progressed through built-in holds. The launch weather officer reported ["favorable and acceptable weather"](https://www.space.com/news/live/artemis-2-nasa-moon-mission-launch-updates-april-1-2026) with an [80 percent chance of acceptable conditions](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/30/nasas-artemis-ii-launch-mission-countdown-begins/), with cloud coverage and high winds being the primary concerns.

## Historic Firsts

The crew represents several milestones in human spaceflight. Victor Glover becomes the [first person of color to travel beyond low Earth orbit](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1). Christina Koch, who holds the record for the longest single spaceflight by a woman at 328 days, becomes the [first woman to reach the lunar vicinity](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1). Jeremy Hansen is the [first non-American to travel beyond Earth orbit](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1).

The mission is also expected to set two distance and speed records. Orion's trajectory is projected to carry the crew more than [250,000 miles from Earth](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1), surpassing Apollo 13's 56-year-old record of 248,655 miles. On reentry, the spacecraft's speed of roughly [25,000 mph will exceed Apollo 10's 1969 record](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1) of 24,791 mph.

## Mission Profile

Artemis II is a lunar flyby mission, not a landing. After liftoff, the [SLS upper stage will raise Orion into a high Earth orbit](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates/) before performing a translunar injection burn. The spacecraft will follow a free-return trajectory, swinging around the Moon and using its gravity to return home. Splashdown is targeted for [approximately April 11 in the Pacific Ocean](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1), where a U.S. Navy vessel will recover the crew and capsule.

The 10-day flight serves as a critical validation of the Orion spacecraft's life support systems, navigation, and communications capabilities with a crew aboard. Its success would clear the path for Artemis III, which [aims to land astronauts on the lunar surface](https://www.space.com/news/live/artemis-2-nasa-moon-mission-launch-updates-april-1-2026).

## What We Don't Know

While launch operations are underway, several uncertainties remain. The SLS rocket has flown only once before, on the uncrewed Artemis I mission in November 2022, and this is its first flight carrying humans. The mission's technical challenges include Orion's heat shield performance during reentry at lunar return velocities. The Artemis program faced repeated delays before reaching this point, including a [liquid hydrogen leak that scrubbed the original February launch window](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/30/nasas-artemis-ii-launch-mission-countdown-begins/) and forced a rollback to the Vehicle Assembly Building for repairs, followed by a [second rollout on March 20](https://www.space.com/news/live/artemis-2-nasa-moon-mission-launch-updates-april-1-2026) after an electrical harness repair.
